one_line_takeaway: A theoretically-grounded recipe (L2 local convex approximation + bounded parameter norm + LiSSA inverse-Hessian estimation) that extends provably-certified, differential-privacy-style machine unlearning from convex models to nonconvex DNNs, including nonconvergence and sequential-request settings, at >10x the speed of retraining but with loose (large-epsilon) certification budgets.
key_finding: The modified Newton update yields a certified, provably-bounded-error unlearning procedure for nonconvex DNNs. Empirically, certified unlearning matches the retrain-from-scratch reference most closely on the forget set while preserving retain/test utility, and removes forget-set information more effectively than the baselines under membership-inference evaluation, all while being over 10x faster than retraining. The ablation shows the local convex approximation and the ||w||<=C constraint are both essential to keeping the error bound small (e.g., the error bound collapses from ~35078 at lambda=0 to ~44 at lambda=1e4 on AllCNN/CIFAR-10), and the LiSSA inverse-Hessian approximation gives ~470x speedup over exact inversion (MLP/MNIST).
core_contribution: The paper extends certified machine unlearning (a rigorous, differential-privacy-derived notion where the unlearned model is provably close in distribution to a model retrained from scratch) from convex models to nonconvex deep neural networks WITHOUT assuming convexity. The authors' own contribution is a set of simple modifications to the standard single-step Newton (influence-function) update that make its approximation error provably bounded for weakly-convex DNN objectives: (1) local convex approximation via an L2 regularization term (H + lambda*I) to bound the inverse-Hessian norm when the objective is nonconvex (lambda_min < 0), and (2) a constraint ||w||_2 <= C enforced through projected gradient descent during training to bound ||w* - w~*||^2 with a worst-case bound independent of the size of the forget set. They pair this with an efficient inverse-Hessian estimator (LiSSA, from prior work) plus a gradient-substitution trick that replaces grad L over the retained set with grad L over the (much smaller) forget set, giving overall complexity O(s p^2 + n_u p) instead of O(n p^2 + p^3), while proving the error bound is preserved (Theorem 3.6). They further extend the certification to two realistic regimes their contribution is NEW for: nonconvergence training (early-stopping, where w* is not an exact minimizer, handled by assuming bounded residual gradient norm G, Proposition 4.1) and sequential unlearning (requests arriving over time, Proposition 4.2, Algorithm 2).
threat_model: Grounded and privacy-oriented. The adversary is a party who wants to infer whether a specific user's data sample was part of the training set of the deployed model (operationalized via state-of-the-art membership inference attacks, Chen et al. 2021), motivated by 'right to be forgotten' regulation (GDPR, CCPA). The defender must, on a deletion request, produce an unlearned model whose OUTPUT DISTRIBUTION is (epsilon, delta)-indistinguishable (Definition 2.1, borrowed from differential privacy) from that of a model retrained without the deleted data, so that no adversary can distinguish 'data was used' from 'data was deleted' beyond the certification budget. The guarantee is achieved by adding calibrated Gaussian noise Y ~ N(0, sigma^2 I) with sigma >= (Delta/epsilon)*sqrt(2 ln(1.25/delta)) where Delta bounds the approximation error (Theorem 2.3).
limitations: (1) The certification depends on Lipschitz constants L and M that are intractable to bound for real DNNs; the authors set them as hyperparameters, which they concede renders the reported certification LEVEL imprecise (only soundness of the bound form is preserved). (2) The achievable certification budgets are loose: Figure 4 shows epsilon values on the order of 1e2-1e3 (far above the classical DP epsilon < 1) are needed to keep test utility high; tight budgets force large noise and degrade utility. (3) The parameter-norm constraint C trades off against original-model accuracy. (4) The error bound is admittedly not tight ('potential to be further tightened'). (5) Experiments are limited to small/medium image classifiers (MLP, AllCNN, ResNet18) on three datasets; the exact-inverse-Hessian ablation only ran on MLP/MNIST due to memory limits. (6) The relearn-time metric required an artificially chosen threshold because the natural threshold gave indistinguishable results.

datasets_used: MNIST (60,000 train / 10,000 test handwritten digits; used with a 3-layer MLP)
CIFAR-10 (50,000 train / 10,000 test color images, 10 classes; used with an AllCNN model)
SVHN (73,257 train / 26,032 test Google Street View house-number images; used with ResNet18)
quantitative_results: Over 10x unlearning-time speedup for certified unlearning vs. retrain-from-scratch across the three DNNs (Figure 2)
470x speedup from LiSSA inverse-Hessian approximation vs. exact inverse (torch.linalg.solve), MLP/MNIST

Ablation error bound (AllCNN/CIFAR-10) drops as lambda increases: 35077.75 (lambda=0), 390.33 (lambda=10), 78.45 (lambda=1e2), 46.96 (lambda=1e3), 43.81 (lambda=1e4); the actual approximation error stays ~26.16-26.51 with the ||w||<=C constraint vs ~46.70-46.89 without it (Table 3)

Utility micro-F1 (Certified vs Retrain reference): MLP/MNIST forget-set Du 97.60 vs 97.20, retain Dr 98.28 vs 98.27, test Dt 97.37 vs 97.19; AllCNN/CIFAR-10 Du 87.83 vs 82.67, Dr 90.68 vs 90.10, Dt 83.04 vs 82.39; ResNet18/SVHN Du 93.73 vs 93.13, Dr 94.61 vs 95.73, Dt 92.94 vs 93.34 (Table 1)

Membership-inference / relearn metrics for Certified vs Retrain: MLP/MNIST relearn-time 24 vs 25, MIA acc 93.22 vs 93.10, MIA AUC 95.28 vs 95.16; AllCNN/CIFAR-10 relearn-time 25 vs 17, MIA acc 78.00 vs 79.82, MIA AUC 87.22 vs 88.71; ResNet18/SVHN relearn-time 9 vs 7, MIA acc 88.63 vs 90.47, MIA AUC 92.18 vs 93.07 (Table 2)
Sequential unlearning: 10,000 SVHN samples deleted from ResNet18 over 10 iterations; gradient norm, error bound, and test F1 stay stable/robust across steps (Figure 3)

Certification-budget study: keeping high test utility requires epsilon on the order of 1e2-1e3 (delta fixed at 0.1 for the epsilon sweep, epsilon=1e3 for the delta sweep), well above classical DP's epsilon<1 (Figure 4)
baselines_compared: Negative Gradient / gradient ascent on forget set (Golatkar et al. 2020)
